coredata.dc:title: How do magnetic, structural, and electronic criteria of aromaticity relate to HOMO – LUMO gap? An evaluation for graphene quantum dot and its derivatives
coredata.dc:creator: Vu, Khanh B.
Le Phuc Nhi, Truong
Vu, Van V.
Tung Ngo, Son
coredata.dc:description: The purpose of our study is to theoretically calculate the HOMO – LUMO gaps and aromaticity indices (HOMA, PDI, FLU, MCBO, PLR, Shannon, and NICS) for graphene quantum dot molecule and its seventeen derivatives. The correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and each aromaticity index was established for the core and petal rings. The linear correlation coefficient for the petal ring is: NICS(1) (0.7181) > PLR (0.6910) > PDI (0.6740) > FLU (0.6098) > MCBO (0.5294) > HOMA (0.3846) > Shannon (0.1498); and that for the core ring is: Shannon (0.4846) > MCBO (0.3765) > PLR (0.3334) > PDI (0.2647) > FLU (0.2356) > NICS (1) (0.2072) > HOMA (0.0529). All calculated aromaticity indices show that the petal ring is more aromatic than the core ring. A better correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and the energetic or electronic delocalization criterion than the structural criterion was observed for the investigated molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Aromaticity indices
Aromaticity criteria
HOMO – LUMO gap

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Foundation for Science and Technology Development

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structures of graphene quantum dot and its seventeen derivatives. Red bonds of substituents are bonded to graphene quantum dot; c and p stand for core and petal rings, respectively.
NICS scan of graphene quantum dot from −4 to 4 Å with 0.2 Å interval at B3LYP/6-311G level of theory.
Linear relationship between aromaticity descriptor and the HOMO – LUMO gap of graphene quantum dot and its derivatives (at B3LYP/6-311G level of theory).
The comparison of linear regression coefficients among the aromaticity indices for core and petal rings (a). Linear relationship between PDI and PLR (b).
HOMO (a) and LUMO (b) of graphene quantum dot. HOMO (c) and LUMO (d) of graphene quantum dot functionalized with –NN-C6H5.

The electron localization function (ELF) of graphene quantum dot (a) and graphene quantum dot functionalized with –NN-C6H5 (b). The ELF values [0–1] are mapped on a blue-green–red color scale indicated on the right of each representation. Blue and red colors indicate no electron and high electron localization, respectively.

The ICSS_zz surface of graphene quantum dot (a) and its derivative with –NN-C6H5 substituent (b) at an isovalue of 0.06. The positive (green) and negative (blue) z-components indicate shielding and de-shielding values when a magnetic field along the z-direction is applied. The distribution of magnetic shielding of graphene quantum dot (c) and its derivative (d) in the line perpendicular to the ring plane and starting from the center of the core ring and petal ring.
Linear regression between NICS with the HOMO – LUMO gap of graphene quantum dot. R2 is the regression coefficient of the linear fitting, and NICS was calculated with different levels of theory.

row.entry: R2 between NICS and the HOMO – LUMO gap

head.ce:title: How do magnetic, structural, and electronic criteria of aromaticity relate to HOMO – LUMO gap? An evaluation for graphene quantum dot and its derivatives

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemical Engineering, School of Biotechnology, International University, Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam (K.B. Vu); Laboratory of Theoretical and Computational Biophysics, Ton Duc Thang University, Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam (S.T. Ngo).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The purpose of our study is to theoretically calculate the HOMO – LUMO gaps and aromaticity indices (HOMA, PDI, FLU, MCBO, PLR, Shannon, and NICS) for graphene quantum dot molecule and its seventeen derivatives. The correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and each aromaticity index was established for the core and petal rings. The linear correlation coefficient for the petal ring is: NICS(1) (0.7181) > PLR (0.6910) > PDI (0.6740) > FLU (0.6098) > MCBO (0.5294) > HOMA (0.3846) > Shannon (0.1498); and that for the core ring is: Shannon (0.4846) > MCBO (0.3765) > PLR (0.3334) > PDI (0.2647) > FLU (0.2356) > NICS (1) (0.2072) > HOMA (0.0529). All calculated aromaticity indices show that the petal ring is more aromatic than the core ring. A better correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and the energetic or electronic delocalization criterion than the structural criterion was observed for the investigated molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dot is zero-dimensional materials and has attracted considerable attention because it offers exceptional characteristics such as low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, chemical stability, and remarkable quantum confinement effect. Therefore, it has been considered to have a variety of applications in bio-imaging [1], temperature sensing [2,3], drug delivery [4–6], cancer therapeutics [7,8], surfactants [9,10], OPV solar cells [11], photodetectors [12], LEDs lighter converters [13], biosensors [14], and photoluminescent materials [15]. Two-dimensional graphene sheet has zero HOMO – LUMO gap because its HOMO and LUMO meets at the Dirac point. Once this 2D graphene sheet shrinks to 0D (quantum dot), its HOMO – LUMO gap opens up and it becomes a semiconductor as a consequence of quantum size effect. The applications of graphene quantum dot for specific purposes mostly depend on its HOMO – LUMO gap; therefore, the structural modification via functionalization to tailor its HOMO – LUMO gap makes them suitable for particular applications [16]. The HOMO – LUMO gap of graphene quantum dot can also be tuned by changing its size; the bigger size leads to a smaller HOMO – LUMO gap and vice versa. The current graphene quantum dot has a HOMO – LUMO gap in a range of 2.2 – 3.1 eV, and it often emits green or blue fluorescence. Graphene quantum dot with a HOMO – LUMO gap narrower than 2.2 eV is not common [17].

There is a variety of methods to measure the aromaticity of molecules. They can be mainly classified into four criteria, according to the used principle: i) energetic criterion such as Dewar resonance energy (DRE) [18], Hess-Schaad resonance energy (HSRE) [19], topological resonance energy (TRE) [20], and isodesmic and homodesmotic stabilization energies (HSE/ISE) [21,22]; ii) magnetic criterion such as the nucleus-independent chemical shift (NICS) [23] and isochemical shielding surface (ICSS) [24]; iii) structural criterion such as harmonic oscillator model of aromaticity (HOMA) [25] and Bird [26]; and iv) electronic delocalization criterion such as multicenter bond order (MCBO) [27], para-delocalization index (PDI) [28], para linear response index (PLR) [29], aromatic fluctuation index (FLU) [30], electron localization function (ELF) [31], average two-center index (ATI) [32], delocalization index (DI and ΔDI) [28], Shannon aromaticity index [33], ring critical point (RCP) [34], and ellipticity of the bond (EL) [35]. Among these aromaticity indices, perhaps, the most widely used magnetic-based index of aromaticity is NICS, proposed by Schleyer and coworkers. It is worth noting that the above-mentioned list of acronyms is extensive but does not fully cover all of the aromaticity indices; more aromaticity indices can be found in the previous publication [36]. The aromaticity is one of the most important concepts in chemistry; however, it does not have a unified definition. As a result, not all of them provide the same ordering by aromaticity of a series of rings or molecules, adding confusion to the field. Therefore, using a set of aromaticity indices is recommended because some of them may not be useful for certain investigated situations [37–41].

It has been found that the magnitude of the paratropic H1NMR chemical shifts was linearly dependent on the magnitude of the HOMO – LUMO gap. The large LUMO-HOMO gap is related to small paratropic shifts and vice versa [42]. The HOMO – LUMO gap may serve as an index to measure the aromaticity, and a larger the HOMO – LUMO gap the higher is the degree of aromaticity of the respective molecule [43–45]. The HOMO – LUMO gaps have been calculated for five-membered heteroaromatic compounds, and there is little correlation (R2 < 0.38) of the HOMO – LUMO gap with any of the structural, energetic, or delocalization criterion for aromaticity [46]. However, in a similar study examining a series of five-membered C4H4X compounds ranging from anti-aromatic to highly aromatic, they found an excellent correlation (R2 = 0.99) between the HOMO – LUMO gap and NICS or aromatic stabilization energy. Recently, the study working on oligomers of benzene, pyrrole, furan, and thiophene has established a good correlation (R2 ≈ 0.958–0.996) between the HOMO – LUMO gap and NICS [47]. Despite the usefulness of the HOMO – LUMO gap as an index for aromaticity, the number of studies examining the relationship between the HOMO – LUMO gap and the aromaticity indices are relatively rare.

In our work, we calculated the HOMO – LUMO gaps and aromaticity indices (HOMA, PDI, FLU, MCBO, PLR, Shannon, and NICS) for graphene quantum dot molecule and its seventeen derivatives. The correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and each aromaticity index was established for the core and petal rings.

The geometries and energies of graphene quantum dot and its seventeen derivatives were fully optimized using Gaussian 09 [48] at B3LYP/6-311G level of theory, followed by the calculations of vibrational frequencies to confirm the minima. NICS(0), NICS(1), NICS(-1), NICS(1.5), NICS(-1.5), NICS(2), NICS(-2) were calculated using the gauge independent atomic orbital (GIAO) method [49] implemented in the Gaussian 09 package. The positive and negative numbers in the parentheses of NICSs are the distances (Å) above and below the centers of the rings, respectively. NICS scan was performed for the petal and core rings from −4 to +4 Å with an interval of 0.2 Å. HOMA, PDI, FLU, MCBO, PLR, Shannon indices were calculated using Multiwfn 3.6 [50]; the output files from Gaussian were used as inputs for running Multiwfn 3.6. Multiwfn is a multifunctional wavefunction analysis program that can be freely downloaded. Unless otherwise stated, the default settings of the program are utilized during our calculations. The structures of graphene quantum dot and its seventeen derivatives are shown in Fig. 1 .

We initially performed the scan of NICS values for the core and petal rings (z ranges from −4 to +4 Å) to properly select the z values for further calculations of NICS, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . The NICS scan data in Fig. 2 imply that the petal ring is more aromatic than the core ring in the range of −2 ≤ z ≤ +2 because negative, positive, and zero values of NICS indicate aromaticity, antiaromaticity, and nonaromaticity, respectively. The observation of the NICS scan suggests that further calculations should be performed with z from −2 to +2 Å.

It has been realized that the NICS value is sensitive with the method and basis set, so we calculated the NICS values in our study at different levels of theory: B3LYP/6-311G, B3LYP/6-311G(d,p), CAM-B3LYP/6-311G, and CAM-B3LYP/6-311G(d,p). The linear regression between NICS (core or petal ring) with the HOMO – LUMO gap of graphene quantum dot was evaluated, and the calculated results are presented in Table 1 . Regardless of the selected methods and basis sets, the correlation between NICS(1) or NICS(-1) and the HOMO – LUMO gap is better than that between NICS(0) and the HOMO – LUMO gap. This observation can be explained that the original NICS(0) contains a lot of σ contributions, and these σ contributions are short ranged. Therefore, by raising the Bq (ghost atom) significantly reduces the σ contaminations. This observation is consistent with the other studies suggested that NICS(0.5) and NICS(1) are better aromaticity descriptors than NICS(0) because the σ contributions are reduced at a distance of 0.5 Å or 1 Å above the ring center [51,52]. Moreover, the HOMO and LUMO are frontier orbitals that relate to the π electrons in graphene quantum dot and its derivatives; as a result, NICS(1) or NICS(-1) has a better relationship with the HOMO – LUMO gap than NICS(0). Moreover, the linear regression coefficients in Table 1 obtained using a variety of levels of theory show that B3LYP is always better than CAM-B3LYP regardless of basis sets used. Therefore, we selected the B3LYP/6-311G level of theory for further calculations.

The linear relationship between each aromaticity index (NICS(0), NICS(1), PLR, PDI, FLU, MCBO, HOMA, or Shannon) with the HOMO – LUMO gap is shown in Fig. 3 . The NICSs of six petal rings were calculated and averaged to give a NICS of the petal ring. Fig. 3 shows that there is a good linear relationship between the HOMO – LUMO gap and each aromaticity index of the petal ring except HOMA and Shannon indices. However, the core ring shows a poor relationship between the HOMO – LUMO gap with all aromaticity indices.

The convergence between the core and petal rings for each aromaticity index with the HOMO – LUMO gap is clearly observed from Fig. 3. In other words, the smaller difference in the value of aromaticity index between the petal ring and the core ring gives the narrower HOMO – LUMO gap and vice versa. According to quantum chemical calculations for graphene quantum dot using the density functional theory, the AdNDP model suggested that the core ring is expected to be more aromatic than the petal rings. However, Clar’s pi-sextet, FLU, PDI, MCI, Iring, NICS(1)zz, and HOMA values reveal that the petal rings are more aromatic than the core ring [53,54]. Our calculations showing in Fig. 3 for eighteen structures are consistent with the latter finding that the petal rings are more aromatic than the core ring. If we go from a narrower to a wider HOMO – LUMO gap, the aromaticity of the core and petal rings increases; however, an increase in aromaticity of the petal ring is more pronounced than that of the core ring. In other words, the divergence of aromaticity between the core and petal rings leads to a higher degree of electron localization and hence a wider HOMO – LUMO gap. On the contrary, the convergence of aromaticity between the core and petal rings leads to preferable electron delocalization and therefore a narrower HOMO – LUMO gap. For the petal ring, the error bars of NICS show that NICS(0) has the smallest standard deviation in comparison with other NICSs (cf. supporting information) because the induced ring current of the petal ring at z = 0 Å is not significantly affected by the substituents on the peripheral positions of graphene quantum dot. Nevertheless, the larger standard deviations of NICS(1) and NICS(−1) originate from the interference of the bulky components of substituents (out of molecular plane) on induced ring current. This interference is not symmetrical as a result of the unsymmetrical geometry of the molecule after peripheral hydrogen atoms are replaced by the bulky substituents. It is also worth noting that for both core and petal rings, the values of NICS(1) are almost similar to those of NICS(-1) (cf. supporting information). This observation implies that both sides of the molecular plane can be selected as NICS(z) or NICS(-z).

The linear regression coefficient (R2) of the HOMO – LUMO gap with each aromaticity index for both petal and core rings is shown in Fig. 4 a. For the petal ring, HOMA and Shannon give the poorest while NICS(1) gives the best linear relationship with the HOMO – LUMO gaps. On another note, Fig. 4b shows that PDI and PLR provide the same degree for measuring the aromaticity because of the good linear relationship between them with R2 = 0.9724 (petal ring) and 0.9751 (core ring). This finding is quite similar to the previous observation finding out that PLR is as useful as PDI in quantitatively measuring the aromaticity with the linear regression coefficient between them being as high as 0.96 [29]. This is reasonable because the definition of PLR has an analogy to PDI with the only difference is that DI is replaced by the condensed linear response kernel (CLRK).

The discrepancy in our study relating to the HOMO – LUMO gap with NICS index and the rest indices can be attributed to the fact that the magnetic criteria (NICS) refer to the effects of the induced ring currents in a perturbed (by external magnetic field) electron density, which can be represented by a series of virtual excitations between frontier molecular orbitals. Those excitations usually involve four HOMO electrons out of the 24 π-electrons of graphene quantum dot, leading to the overall pattern of current–density with the E2u → E1g HOMO − LUMO excitations to the diatropic perimeter current (outer circuit in graphene quantum dot) and the E2u → E2u HOMO − LUMO + 1 excitations giving the weaker paratropic current (inner circuit in graphene quantum dot) [55]. Thus, the magnetic response in aromatic systems is mainly “shaped” by a fraction of π -electrons (8/24 π in graphene quantum dot) in the system because the σ contributions are significantly reduced at a distance of 1 Å above the ring center. As a result, it is generally not comparable with the structural and electronic criteria of aromaticity because both structural and electronic criteria are determined by all the electrons in the systems. As mentioned above, since the magnetic response mainly involves frontier molecular orbitals, a better correlation between NICS(1) index with the HOMO-LUMO gap compared with other indices is reasonably expected.

It is worth mentioning that a better acceptor substituent (–C6H5 < -C6H5Cl < -C6H5Br < –NN-C6H5) on the periphery of graphene quantum dot leads to a lower HOMO – LUMO gap. We show the HOMO and LUMO of graphene quantum dot (the largest HOMO – LUMO gap) and graphene quantum dot with –NN-C6H5 substituent (the smallest HOMO – LUMO gap) in Fig. 5 . The energy level of graphene quantum dot: HOMO (-5.726 eV), LUMO (−1.643 eV). The energy level of graphene quantum dot with –NN-C6H5: HOMO (−4.357 eV), LUMO (−3.503 eV). This substituent lowers LUMO energy via accepting electron. Making the molecule becoming more electrophile, this electron acceptance effect is similar to the doping effect in conjugated polymers, which switches conjugated polymers from semiconductor to conductor. The electron localization functions (ELF) of graphene quantum dot and its derivative with –NN-C6H5 were calculated and are shown in Fig. 6 . The ELF maps show that electrons in graphene quantum dot tend to be more localized than those in its derivative with –NN-C6H5. In other words, the smaller HOMO – LUMO gap is due to better electron delocalization. It is worth mentioning that our ELF analysis only shows the electron density and does not have any information about sigma and pi bonds separately. The separation of the ELF into its pi and sigma components allows one to better describe the aromaticity of classical ring and new aromatic molecules [56].

To understand the difference between graphene quantum dot and its derivative with –NN-C6H5 substituent in terms of magnetic shielding distribution, we performed the calculation for the isochemical shielding surface (ICSS) and then extract the magnetic shielding distribution along the z-axis for the core ring and petal ring. The ICSS_zz surface and the variation of magnetic shielding in the line perpendicular to the ring plane and starting from the center of the core ring and petal ring are shown in Fig. 7 . For graphene quantum dot, the core ring (Fig. 7c) is de-shielding at z = 0 (Bohr), begins shielding at z ~ 1.5 (Bohr), and has a maximum shielding at z ~ 3.7 (Bohr). After that, the shielding gradually decreases with the higher z values. This observation is quite different from the benzene molecule where shielding is always observed with any z values. In contrast to the core ring, the petal ring shows the strongest shielding at z = 0 and gradually decreases with the higher z values. This observation suggests that the petal rings have better aromaticity than the core ring at the same z value, which is consistent with NICS, FLU, PDI, HOMA, MCBO, Shannon, and PLR calculations. For graphene quantum dot with –NN-C6H5 substituent, the distribution curves of magnetic shielding (Fig. 7d) of the core and petal rings are similar to those of graphene quantum dot, except that the core and petal rings have a maximum deshielding and a maximum shielding at z ~ 1.0 (Bohr), respectively. Moreover, at the same z value, the shielding values of the core and petal rings of graphene quantum dot with –NN-C6H5 substituent are much lower than those of graphene quantum dot. This indicates that electrons are less localized in the core and petal rings of graphene quantum dot with –NN-C6H5 substituent than those of graphene quantum dot, and this likely leads to a much lower HOMO – LUMO gap of the former than that of the latter.

The correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and each aromaticity index (HOMA, PDI, FLU, MCBO, PLR, Shannon, or NICS) has been established for the core and petal rings in graphene quantum dot molecule and its seventeen derivatives. In comparison with the core ring, all calculated aromaticity indices show that the petal ring is more aromatic and has much better correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and each aromaticity index. For the petal ring, NICS(1) also gives the best linear relationship with the HOMO – LUMO gap compared with other aromaticity indices. A better correlation between the HOMO – LUMO gap and the energetic or electronic delocalization criterion than the structural criterion was observed for the investigated molecules. Our study also points out that PDI and PLR provide the same degree for measuring the aromaticity because of the good linear relationship between them.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is funded by Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 104.05-2018.47.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemphys.2020.110951.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent graphene quantum dots for biosensing and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Fan, S. Li, F. Yuan, L. Fan, Fluorescent graphene quantum dots for biosensing and bioimaging, RSC Advances, 5 (2015) 19773-19789.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene Quantum Dots from Mangifera indica: Application in Near-Infrared Bioimaging and Intracellular Nanothermometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Kumawat, M. Thakur, R.B. Gurung, R. Srivastava, Graphene Quantum Dots from Mangifera indica: Application in Near-Infrared Bioimaging and Intracellular Nanothermometry, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 5 (2017) 1382-1391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Islam, An ultrafast quantum thermometer from graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Sehrawat, Abid, S.S. Islam, An ultrafast quantum thermometer from graphene quantum dots, Nanoscale Advances, 1 (2019) 1772-1783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Milk-derived multi-fluorescent graphene quantum dot-based cancer theranostic system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Thakur, A. Mewada, S. Pandey, M. Bhori, K. Singh, M. Sharon, M. Sharon, Milk-derived multi-fluorescent graphene quantum dot-based cancer theranostic system, Materials Science and Engineering: C, 67 (2016) 468-477.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dot assisted translocation of drugs into a cell membrane

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xue, Q. Sun, L. Zhang, Z. Kang, L. Liang, Q. Wang, J.-W. Shen, Graphene quantum dot assisted translocation of drugs into a cell membrane, Nanoscale, 11 (2019) 4503-4514.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid oleic acid-graphene quantum dot vesicles for drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S. Boruah, D. Chowdhury, Hybrid Oleic Acid-Graphene Quantum Dot Vesicles for Drug Delivery, ChemistrySelect, 4 (2019) 4347-4354.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of biosurfactant-based graphene quantum dot conjugate as a novel and fluorescent theranostic tool for cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Bansal, J. Singh, U. Kumari, I.P. Kaur, R.P. Barnwal, R. Kumar, S. Singh, G. Singh, M. Chatterjee, Development of biosurfactant-based graphene quantum dot conjugate as a novel and fluorescent theranostic tool for cancer, Int J Nanomedicine, 14 (2019) 809-818.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional graphene quantum dots for combined photothermal and photodynamic therapy coupled with cancer cell tracking applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Thakur, M.K. Kumawat, R. Srivastava, Multifunctional graphene quantum dots for combined photothermal and photodynamic therapy coupled with cancer cell tracking applications, RSC Advances, 7 (2017) 5251-5261.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The synthesis of amphiphilic luminescent graphene quantum dot and its application in miniemulsion polymerization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zeng, X. Wang, Y.-H. Yu, L. Zhang, W. Shafi, X. Huang, Z. Cheng, The synthesis of amphiphilic luminescent graphene quantum dot and its application in miniemulsion polymerization, Journal of Nanomaterials, 2016 (2016).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aqueous synthesis of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots and their application as surfactants for preparing of fluorescent polymer microspheres

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. He, Z. Li, R. Zhao, Y. Lu, L. Shi, J. Liu, X. Dong, F. Xi, Aqueous synthesis of amphiphilic graphene quantum dots and their application as surfactants for preparing of fluorescent polymer microspheres, Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects, 563 (2019) 77-83.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of Dye-sensitized Solar Cells Efficiency Using Graphene Quantum Dots as Photoanode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Kim, B. Lee, Y.J. Kim, S.W. Hwang, Enhancement of Dye-sensitized Solar Cells Efficiency Using Graphene Quantum Dots as Photoanode, Bulletin of the Korean Chemical Society, 40 (2019) 56-61.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-broadband photodetectors based on epitaxial graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. El Fatimy, A. Nath, D. Kong Byoung, K. Boyd Anthony, L. Myers-Ward Rachael, M. Daniels Kevin, M.M. Jadidi, E. Murphy Thomas, D.K. Gaskill, P. Barbara, Ultra-broadband photodetectors based on epitaxial graphene quantum dots, in: Nanophotonics, 2018, pp. 735.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Yellow fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a phosphor for white tunable light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yin, J. Zhou, W. Li, J. Zhang, L. Wang, Yellow fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a phosphor for white tunable light-emitting diodes, RSC Advances, 9 (2019) 9301-9307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots-based electrochemical biosensor for catecholamine neurotransmitters detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Baluta, A. Lesiak, J. Cabaj, Graphene Quantum Dots-based Electrochemical Biosensor for Catecholamine Neurotransmitters Detection, Electroanalysis, 30 (2018) 1781-1790.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence mechanism in graphene quantum dots: Quantum confinement effect and surface/edge state

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, J. Wang, H. Wan, Y. Zhang, Y. Ning, B. Yang, Photoluminescence mechanism in graphene quantum dots: Quantum confinement effect and surface/edge state, Nano Today, 13 (2017) 10-14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Systematic bandgap engineering of graphene quantum dots and applications for photocatalytic water splitting and CO2 reduction
